stem: For \( n \ge 1 \), define \( f_n(x) = \left(\dfrac{x}{[[c]]}\right)^{n} \) on \( [-[[b]], [[b]]] \). Since \( |x| \le [[b]] \), we have \( |f_n(x)| \le M_n = \left(\dfrac{[[b]]}{[[c]]}\right)^{n} \) for every \( x \) in this interval, and \( \displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} M_n \) converges (a geometric series with ratio \( \dfrac{[[b]]}{[[c]]} < 1 \)), so by the Weierstrass M-test \( \displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} f_n(x) \) converges uniformly on \( [-[[b]],[[b]]] \). What is the value of \( \displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} M_n \), the majorizing series that certifies this?
solution: The majorant series \( \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} M_n \) is geometric with first term \( M_1 = \dfrac{[[b]]}{[[c]]} \) and ratio \( r=\dfrac{[[b]]}{[[c]]} \), so \( \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} M_n = \dfrac{r}{1-r} = \dfrac{[[b]]/[[c]]}{1-[[b]]/[[c]]} = \dfrac{[[b]]}{[[c]]-[[b]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is only the first term \( M_1 \) of the series, not the sum of the whole series.
This inverts the ratio \( b/c \) itself.
This uses \( \frac{1}{1-r} \), dropping the first-term factor \( r \) (this is the sum starting the series from \( M_0=1 \), not \( M_1 \)).
This computes \( 1-r \) directly instead of the sum \( \frac{r}{1-r} \).
This is off by one from the correct sum.
This is off by one from the correct sum.

stem: For \( n \ge 1 \), define \( f_n(x) = \dfrac{x}{(n+[[a]])(n+[[a]]+1)} \) on \( [-[[d]], [[d]]] \). Since \( |x| \le [[d]] \), we have \( |f_n(x)| \le M_n = \dfrac{[[d]]}{(n+[[a]])(n+[[a]]+1)} \) for every \( x \) in this interval, and \( \displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} M_n \) converges (it telescopes), so by the Weierstrass M-test \( \displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} f_n(x) \) converges uniformly on \( [-[[d]],[[d]]] \). What is the value of \( \displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} M_n \), the majorizing series that certifies this?
solution: The \( N \)th partial sum telescopes: \( \sum_{n=1}^{N} \dfrac{[[d]]}{(n+[[a]])(n+[[a]]+1)} = [[d]]\left(\dfrac{1}{[[a]]+1} - \dfrac{1}{N+[[a]]+1}\right) \). As \( N \to \infty \) the tail term vanishes, leaving \( \displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} M_n = \dfrac{[[d]]}{[[a]]+1} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This omits the factor \( d \), the bound on \( |x| \) that scales every \( M_n \).
This inverts the correct ratio, multiplying by \( a+1 \) instead of dividing by it.
This squares the denominator instead of leaving it linear.
This is off by one from the correct sum.
This is off by one from the correct sum.
